expectations.evidence: Substring appears in many places, e.g. line 4 ('rows under `ripple_param_name="Kay_ripple_detection_v2"`'), line 32, line 35, line 91, line 114, etc.
Line 172: '`.delete()` is `cautious_delete` — it will block on team permissions...'. Also line 297: '`.delete()` on `SpyglassMixin` is `cautious_delete`...'

The literal string `destructive_operations.md` does not appear anywhere in response.md (verified by grep). The response references `ripple_pipeline.md` (line 107) but never names `destructive_operations.md`, despite the executor having read that reference file.

expectations.text: Uses the correct field name `ripple_param_name` (singular), not `ripple_params_name`
expectations.evidence: All occurrences use `ripple_param_name`; grep for `ripple_params_name` returns no matches.

expectations.text: Scopes the cleanup to RippleTimesV1 rows with the affected `ripple_param_name`, not all RippleTimesV1 rows
expectations.evidence: Line 35: `affected = RippleTimesV1 & {"ripple_param_name": bad_name}`. Line 164: `stale = RippleTimesV1 & {"ripple_param_name": bad_name}`. Option B's delete is performed on `stale.delete()` (line 177), scoped to the bad_name restriction. Re-populate is scoped via the saved `affected_keys` list (lines 139-142, 189-190), explicitly warning against widening scope.

expectations.text: Explicitly lists what is unaffected — LFP, LFPBand, RippleLFPSelection, position, decoding branches not downstream of the affected ripple times
expectations.evidence: Lines 229-239 'Unaffected siblings / upstream branches' enumerate LFPV1, LFPBandV1, LFPBandSelection, RippleLFPSelection, TrodesPosV1/DLCPosV1, PositionOutput, SpikeSortingV1/CurationV1 as unaffected. Decoding is implied via 'parallel pipelines, not in the ripple FK chain'.

expectations.text: Recommends cautious_delete (not super_delete or a manual drop) and routes to destructive_operations.md
expectations.evidence: Recommends `.delete()` (which the response notes is `cautious_delete` on SpyglassMixin) and explicitly warns against `super_delete` ('don't reach for `super_delete` to silence the `PermissionError`', line 181-182; 'coordinate, don't bypass', line 299). However the response never routes to `destructive_operations.md` — it only cites `ripple_pipeline.md` (line 107). Half of the assertion is met, half is not; per grader rules with no partial credit, this fails.

expectations.text: Flags editing a params row in place as an anti-pattern and recommends inserting a new `ripple_param_name` entry for future runs
expectations.evidence: Line 261-263 'Never `update1()` a `*Params` row when anything downstream has been populated against it. The pattern silently corrupts provenance...'. Line 271-279 '**Default workflow for any parameter change: insert a new `*_param_name` row.** Choose a name that encodes what changed (e.g. `Kay_ripple_detection_v2_speed10`)...'. Lines 209-216 likewise frame in-place mutation as the bug being recovered from.

claims.claim: Existing RippleTimesV1 rows under the edited param name have stale provenance and DataJoint will not flag them automatically.
claims.evidence: Matches the expected_output narrative — DataJoint's name->blob->downstream rows contract makes in-place params edits silently break consistency. Response correctly explains this in lines 4-12 and 215-216.

claims.claim: `speed_threshold` lives nested inside `ripple_detection_params`, not at the top of `ripple_param_dict`.
claims.evidence: Cited at ripple/v1/ripple.py:219-224. Consistent with the Spyglass ripple pipeline structure documented in references.

claims.claim: `.delete()` on a SpyglassMixin table is `cautious_delete` and will block on team-permission grounds for sessions belonging to other lab members.
claims.evidence: Consistent with SpyglassMixin.cautious_delete behavior documented in spyglass/utils/dj_mixin.py and the destructive_operations.md reference.

claims.claim: Re-populating with a bare {'ripple_param_name': ...} restriction will widen scope to every eligible upstream combination.
claims.evidence: Standard DataJoint populate semantics — populate(key) restricts to the join of key with the key_source; a partial restriction does not narrow to the originally-populated rows. The response correctly recommends building per-key populate calls from the saved `affected_keys` list.

claims.claim: Raising the speed threshold loosens the immobility filter and keeps more events; lowering tightens it.
claims.evidence: ripple_detection thresholds the speed trace and accepts events below the threshold (immobility); a higher threshold admits more samples and therefore more candidate ripple events. Cited at ripple_detection/core.py:326-329.

claims.claim: Recovery options A (revert + new name) and B (delete + recompute under same name) are both valid.
claims.evidence: Both restore consistency between the params row and downstream rows. Option A preserves history (preferred when collaborators may have used the old threshold); Option B is acceptable when the new threshold is the canonical version. Aligns with the expected_output recommendation pattern (cautious_delete + repopulate is essentially Option B; recommending a new ripple_param_name aligns with Option A).

suggestions.reason: This is a literal-string match on a filename. The response gave a substantively correct answer (cautious_delete recommended, super_delete warned against, anti-pattern flagged, scope correctly bounded) but failed the assertion solely because it did not name the reference file by filename. If the goal is to verify the assistant routed users to the right reference, consider an OR over markers like `destructive_operations`, `cautious_delete`, and the specific guidance content; if the goal is to verify a literal cross-link, keep the assertion but make the gap discriminating (e.g. require an inspect-before-delete pattern in addition).

suggestions.assertion: Recommends cautious_delete (not super_delete or a manual drop) and routes to destructive_operations.md
suggestions.reason: Compound assertion that conflates two checks (recommendation AND routing). The response cleanly satisfies the recommendation half but fails the routing half. Splitting into two assertions would let graders score the substantive quality (cautious_delete + super_delete warning) separately from the routing/citation discipline.

No assertion explicitly checks that the response distinguishes 'Option A (preserve history via new param name)' from 'Option B (delete + recompute under same name)' — this is a meaningful workflow distinction the response handles well, and a weaker answer that only proposed one path would still satisfy most current assertions.

eval_feedback.overall: The substantive content of this answer is strong and matches the expected_output well. Two of the eight failures are driven by a single issue — the response did not literally cite `destructive_operations.md` even though it did everything that file would have told the user to do. Consider splitting compound assertions or weakening literal-filename checks to focus on substantive guidance.
